The four that made it: ZERO ZERO TWO · Smash Society · Big Dink Energy · K'CHN
The Drop · Special Edition
The Playoff Picture
By The Society Desk

Four teams left, one that scares the other three

ZERO ZERO TWO carried an unbeaten regular season and a plus-319 point differential into the bracket. Behind them, Smash Society, Big Dink Energy and K'CHN are separated by a single match point and a lot of nerve.

Six weeks of regular season are in the books, and the math has sorted itself into a familiar shape: one juggernaut, one clear challenger, and two teams that spent the year proving the standings can lie to you. The top four are locked. What happens next is not.

Seeded first, ZERO ZERO TWO didn't just win — they refused to lose a match, going the entire regular season without dropping a weekly fixture and outscoring the division by 319 points. Their reward is the four-seed and a semifinal against K'CHN, a team they've already handled but never quite buried. On the other side, second-seeded Smash Society meets Big Dink Energy in the kind of matchup that looks lopsided on paper and plays close on the court.

"A plus-319 differential isn't a lead. It's a warning label."

Below is the full teardown: seeds, the head-to-head grid, side-by-side charts, both semifinal matchups, and a roster-by-roster look at who's carrying each team — plus the question every captain in this division is quietly asking, which is whether to win it in men's, women's, or mixed.

Head to Head

Regular-season games won between the top four. Row team's result vs. column team.

🎯 ZZ2👊 Smash😤 Big Dink🤌 K'CHN
🎯 ZERO ZERO TWO—8–0 W3–1 W3–1 W
👊 Smash Society0–8 L—3–1 W4–0 W
😤 Big Dink Energy1–3 L1–3 L—4–4 T
🤌 K'CHN1–3 L0–4 L4–4 T—

The tell: ZERO ZERO TWO swept Smash Society 8–0 across their meetings — the only time the No. 2 team was blanked all year. Meanwhile Big Dink and K'CHN split their series down the middle, 4–4, which is exactly the kind of dead heat the bracket can't replicate.

The Semifinals

Semifinal I · Collision of Extremes

🎯
ZERO ZERO TWO
① Top Seed · 10–0–2
3–1
Season series ZZ2
🤌
K'CHN
④ Four Seed · 6–5–1

On paper this is the mismatch of the round: the unbeaten top seed against the team that backed into the four-hole on point differential. ZERO ZERO TWO scored 738 points this season and allowed 419; K'CHN scored 594 and allowed 574, a razor margin that says every night was a coin flip. When these two met, the coin came up ZZ2 three times out of four.

But K'CHN is not a soft out. Guilbert Balmaceda has quietly logged a 6–2 games record, Patrick captains a 4–1 mark, and the roster's ceiling is real when Katrina Nicasio and Vernice Carag are in rhythm. The problem is matching ZZ2's depth: Shay C (8–0), Judy DV (4–0) and Lara P (7–1) form a top of the lineup that hasn't been solved yet.

Desk read: K'CHN needs to steal the women's and mixed games to have a path. History says ZZ2 closes it out — but a 4–4 team knows how to hang around, and one stolen set changes the temperature fast.

Semifinal II · The Even-Money Fight

👊
Smash Society
② Two Seed · 7–4–1
3–1
Season series Smash
😤
Big Dink Energy
③ Three Seed · 6–4–2

The bracket's best matchup lives here. Smash Society owns the season series 3–1 and a +154 differential, but Big Dink Energy is the only top-four team with a winning-adjacent knack for staying attached — a +46 differential and a roster that goes ten deep. The gap between the two-seed and the three-seed is one match point.

Smash's case is simple and terrifying: Ryan Hom, Annie Kang and Phoebe Borsum all went a perfect 8–0 in the games sampled, a trio that can win a men's, a women's and a mixed set without ever leaving the court. Big Dink counters with the division's feel-good climb — Jobeth Zapata (5–1) surged up the leaderboard, and captain Richard Hak steadied a 4–2 mark with Eli Henry right beside him.

Desk read: If Smash's big three hold serve, this is over early. If Big Dink can drag two of the three into deuce and win the fourth game, the upset is genuinely live. This is the one to watch.

Workhorse

The most complete team in the division and it isn't especially close. ZERO ZERO TWO wins the women's game outright — their women went a combined 8-for-8 in same-gender sets — and their mixed depth (28–4 across the roster) means there's no soft seat to attack. Kyle U (6–2) and Anthony B (5–2) give the men's lineup enough to hold.

Undefeated

Three players, zero losses between them. Ryan Hom, Annie Kang and Phoebe Borsum are the reason Smash is the only team that can plausibly meet ZERO ZERO TWO in the final — any two of them can win any format. The drop-off comes after: Devin Carroll (6–2) and Kai Pylkkanen (5–3) are solid, but the roster leans on its top three carrying the water.

Steadies it

The deepest roster in the field and the one most dependent on getting its rotation right. Big Dink's men are the strength — a combined 6-for-8 in men's sets — while the women's lineup ran hot and cold at 4-for-8. Jobeth Zapata's climb up the leaderboard (a +9 rank jump) is the storyline; if she and Eli Henry keep their mixed form, Big Dink can push Smash to the brink.

Volume F

The four-seed earned its spot in the trenches: a +20 differential and a 4–4 season split with Big Dink say every match was decided late. Guilbert Balmaceda logged the most games of anyone on the roster and captain Patrick posted the team's best rate at 4–1. The vulnerability is the women's format, where K'CHN went just 2-for-8 — a number ZZ2 will target relentlessly.

Katrina Nicasio & Vernice Carag Women's1–0
Guilbert & Patrick Men's1–1
Katrina Nicasio & Philip Cadelina Mixed1–1
Patrick & Vernice Carag Mixed1–0

Gender or Mixed?

Where each contender actually wins

Games won % by format among the top four (bubble size = games played). Higher and to the right is better.

The division's quiet strategic question is whether titles are won in single-gender play or in mixed — and the top four don't agree on the answer. Mixed is simply where the volume is: the four contenders played 125 mixed games against 31 women's and 32 men's. But volume isn't the same as value.

ZERO ZERO TWO and Smash Society are built on their women's games — both went a perfect 8-for-8 and 7-for-7 respectively in same-gender women's sets, a level nobody else touches. Big Dink Energy flips it: their edge is men's play (6-for-8), while their women's lineup lagged at 4-for-8. K'CHN, uniquely, is a mixed-first team — their best format (56%) is mixed, because their single-gender results (2-for-8 women's, 4-for-8 men's) gave them little to lean on.

"The read: mixed decides the volume, but women's doubles decides the ceiling."

That matters for the bracket. ZZ2 and Smash can win a playoff match by protecting the women's set and splitting mixed. The chasers can't — Big Dink has to win the men's game and steal a mixed, and K'CHN has to turn the whole night into a mixed shootout and hope the coin lands its way. Whoever controls the women's court is likely holding the trophy.

By The Society Desk. Team records and point differentials from the six-week regular-season standings. Individual ratings, format splits and pairing records from the current player leaderboard. Semifinals follow the standard 1v4 / 2v3 bracket. Live interactive edition — refreshed as the bracket plays out.
